content.full_text:
California
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornmission
Mr. Philip Mering
court Plaza Building
901 H street, Suite 604
sacramento, cA 9 5814
l4arch 6, 1991
Re: Your Request for Informal- Assistance
Our File No. f-91-091
Dear Mr. Mering:
As the city Attorney of Is]-eton you are seekingT general
advice on behalf of nernbers of the city's council and planning
comrnission regarding their duties and respons ibi I ities under the
conf l ict-of-interest 
provisions of the Po1itical Reform Act (the
"Act"1.1 We provide you with advice as the authorized
representative of the council and commission, Pursuant to
Relulation 18329 (copy enclosed). Because your request.for advice
does not refer to a specific pending governnental decision, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance only.
lnegu:.ation rsrzs 111 .12
The f o]lowing advice is based uPon the facts provided in your
Ietter of February 5, LggL and in a telephone conversation on
February 22, ]-99L.
OUESTION
If a pl-anning commissioner owns property in, or operates a
busj.ness fron within, the city's rrhistoric district,rt 
is the
comrnj.ssioner precluded fron participating in decisions concerning
the district 
that pertain to the city's facade review program or
1 Go.r".r,."nt code sections 81ooo-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government code unless othen^rise lndicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 California code of Regulations section
18ooo, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the california code of Regulations.
2 _^
- Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(section 83114,' Regulation 18329(c) (3). )
428 J Street, Suite 80O . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916)322.5660


File No. I -91-091
Page 2
the city's nuisance abatement ordinance? If a menber of the city
counci 1/ redevelopment agency owns property within the district, 
is
rre or she precluded fron participating in decisions concerning an
appeal from the planning commissj,on under application of the
city's nuisance abatement ordinance?
CONCLUSION
cenerally, a planning cornmissioner who owns property j'n or
operates a business lrithin the city's rrhistoric districttt 
may not
pirticipate 
in a cornmission decision concerning the district 
if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision wj,11 result in a
material financial inpact on a fj,nancial interest of the
commissioner. A metnber of the city council/redevelopnent agency
is sirnilarly prohibited frorn participating in an appeal- from the
planning commission under the nuisance abatement ordinance if the
decision will result in a material financial inpact on a financial
interest of the councilmember.
FACTS
Two metnbers of the City of Isleton planning comrnission own
property within the city's four-bIock trhistoric district-"
Froperty oerners within the district 
are eligible for facade grants
to provide assistance in the irnprovement of store fronts. 
The
grant program, managed by the Sacramento county Housing and
neaeveloprnent Agency, requires applicants to subnit facade designs
to the city'= pianning cornmission for review for compliance with
design review standards adopted by the city's redeveloprnent
ag".,iy. The planning comrnission forwards its reconmendations to
the ttousing a;d Redevelopment Agency, which retains final approval'
authority for allocation of available grant funds.
one menber of the City of fsleton city council owns property
within the tihistoric district.r' 
The city's nuisance abatement
ordinance prohibits the uraintenance of structures in a
deterioratld condition. Enforcenent of the ordinance calls for a
hearing before the city planning conrnission and a right of appeal
to the city council. itrL application of this ordinance is city-
wide, but inany of the buitdinqs regul-ated under the ordinance are
located within the historic district.
ANALYSIS
A public official 
may not make, participate in, or.use.his or
her official 
position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official 
has a financial interest. 
(Section 87100') A
p"ffi" official 
has a financial interest in a decision if it is
ieasonably foreseeable that the decision wiIl have a rnaterial
iinanciaf'effect, 
distinguishabte from its effect on the public
generall,y, on the offj-ciaI, a member of his or her irnrnediate
ianily, ir o.t any real property in which the off icj'a1 has a direct
or ini j-rect inteiest ,oith- one thousand ($r,OOO) or more. (Section
87103 (b) . )


File No. I -91- 091
Page 3
As public officiafs 
(Section 82048), members of the city's
planning commission and city council must disqualify thernselves
from any decisions \.rhich !ri11 have a reasonably foreseeable
material f j-nancial effect, distinguishable from the public
generally, on them, their real property interests (section
87103(b)), or a source of income (Section 87103(c)).
Foreseeable ttaterial Finaacial Effect
The effect of a decisj-on is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantiaL likeLihood that it wj-II occur. While certainty
is not regui-red, an effect that is rnerely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Downev Cares v. Downev Cornmunitv
Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.ApP.3d 983i In re Thorner (1975)
l FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act, however, does seek to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(llitt v. Morro$/
(L977) 7O CaI.App.3d 817, 823.)
Decisions reqardinq facade reviet,
Because the analysis of this issue j-s fact specific, we
provide you only with the following general guidance. Under
iegulation La702.1 (copy encl,osed), when a planning commissioner
who owns property within the historic district 
submits an
application for a facade grant, a reasonably foreseeable material
financiat effect woutd appear to be present. This is so because
his or her real property interest lrouId be involved in a decision
that involved the issuance, denial, or revocation of a license,
pernit or other land use entitlenent authorizing a specific use or
uses of such property. (Regulation ta7o2.1(a) (3) (c).) 
A material
financial inpact would also appear to be present for the
cornmissj-oner operating a business on property o!'/ned within the
district, 
because a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $1,ooo or nore would be involved
in a decision directly before the official's 
agency. (Regulation
1a702.1(a) (2). )
When applications for facade grants are made to the
cornrnission by other property olrners within the district, 
the
officials' 
real property interests are indirectly involved in the
decision. Materiality determinations are subject to analysis under
Regulation La702.3 (copy enclosed). fn part, this reg'ulation
provides that the deternination of whether the effect of a
decision is naterial depends on the distance between the
officiat's 
real property and the boundaries of the proPerty which
is the subject of the decision.s The specific facts of the facade
3    The effect of a decisiOn is presumed material when the
official′ s property interests are located within 300 feet of the
subject of the decision (Regu■ation ■8702.3(a)(■))・  Certain
financial thresholds when the officia■
′s property interests are
located within 2500 feet (Regulation 
■8702.3(a)(3)), and other
considerations when the official′
s property interests are located
beyond 2500 feet (RegulatiOn 
■8702.3(b))′  must be examined as
well.


File No. I-91-091
Page 4
grant application as applied to the regulation's criteria
deterrnine whether a disgualifying conflict results. 
rf you
conclude that the f j,nancial threshoLds of the regulation have been
met, a disqualifyinq conflict would exist.
PubIic qenerallv exception
An official 
otherwise disqualified nay neverthel-ess
participate in a decision if the effect of the decision on the
official's 
interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the
general public; the decision must affect the official's 
interest
in substantialLy the same manner as it would affect a significant
segment of the public. 
(Regulation 18703, copy enclosed. )
Because all residents of the jurisdiction are constituents of
the official, 
these residents in the agency's jurisdiction
comprise the rrpublic. r' (In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC ops. 1; Tn re
Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, copies enclosed.) For the public
genera]]y exception to apply to the issue you have raised, a
decision on a facade grant application must impact a significant
segment of the population of the City of Isleton in substantially
the same manner as it would the individual pl,anning commissioner.
(Eirsch Advice Letter, No. A-90-196, copy enclosed.) This
requires analysis of, and is obviously dependent upon, the facts
part j-cuIar to the situation.
Decisions reqardinq nuisance abatement
The analysis of this issue is also fact specific, so we
provide you with the following general guidance only. 
Under
iegulation 1a702.1, a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect would appear to be Present in any planning commission
decisions under the city's nuisance abatement ordinance involving
a property o$/ned by a planning cornmissioner. A similar inpact
would appear to be present if the property subject to the action
was owned by a member of the city council and the planning
cornnj,ssion decision was appealed to the city council. In both
instances, the official 
owning property rrithin the district 
would
have his or her real property interest directly involved in a de-
cision to issue, deny, or revoke a license, permit or other land
use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of such prop-
erty. 
(Regulation La702.1 (a) (3) (c).) 
A naterial financial impact
would also appear to be present for the councilmember operating a
business on property oqrned within the district, 
because a business
entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investnent of
$1,ooo or more would be involved in a decision directly before the
official's 
agency. (Regulation L8702.1(a) (2).)
when the planning co:nmission and city council, consider
actions under the city's nuisance abatement ordinance involving
properties within the district 
owned by others, the officials'
ieal property interests are indirectly involved in the decision,


File No. I -91-O91
Page 5
and materiality deterninations are subject to analysis under
Regulation 1a7O2.3, as discussed above. whether the effect of a
nuisance abatement decision involving one property within the
district will rnaterially impact another property owned by an
official, within the district 
in part depends on the distance
between the offj-cial's real property and the boundaries of the
property which is the subject of the action. 
The specific facts
of the nuisance abatement action, as applied to the reg"ulation's
criteria, 
would determine whether a disqualifying conflict
results. 
If you conclude that a disqualifying conflict exists
because the financial thresholds of the regulation have been met,
a consideration of the public generally exception, as discussed
prevj-ousJ-y, would also be appropriate.
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regardinq this
matter, or if you wish to supplement your advice request with
specific facts pertaining to a pending cornrnission decision, please
contact ne at (916) 322-5901.
sincerel-y,
Scott Hallabrin
sH: JsR: ken
Enc I osures
Legal Division
. Rothnan
